patterns.description: The agent re-runs nearly identical probe commands across consecutive turns against slightly different interpreters or paths without first performing the discovery step that would have told it where the target package actually lives. Each retry observes the same failure mode before the agent finally pivots to the correct diagnostic — a search that should have preceded the first attempt rather than followed the third.

evidence_refs.excerpt: pip show astroid ... && python -c "import astroid; print('ok')"
python -c "import astroid; from pylint.testutils import ..." (identical body)
python -c "import astroid; from pylint.testutils import ..."

patterns.description: The agent continues reading progressively deeper into the call chain (many Grep/Read calls descending through helper functions) well after it has already identified the actual bug site. The extra exploration does not meaningfully refine the diagnosis and partly serves as rationalization/re-derivation of a conclusion reached earlier — or, in the worst case, of a conclusion it ultimately imports from an external reference rather than deriving itself.

evidence_refs.excerpt: I'm weighing different approaches to fix the coordinate attribute merging issue. Option 1 would require modifying the function signature, option 2 doesn't work because the lambda would grab the wrong attributes, option 3 is a post-processin
Read computation.py offset=1154 limit=70
Read computation.py offset=454 limit=50 (re-reading same range)
Read computation.py offset=454 limit=60
Read computation.py offset=280 limit=60

def collect_dict_values(
    objects: Iterable[Mapping | Any], keys: Iterable, fill_value: object = None
The issue is on line 1863. When `keep_attrs=True`, the lambda `lambda attrs, context: getattr(x, "attrs", {})` returns the attrs of `x` for ALL variables, including coordinate variables.

patterns.description: The agent wraps nearly every simple shell command (grep, sed, cat, one-line python) in the Monitor background-task tool, then issues a follow-up `sleep N && cat /tmp/.../tasks/*.output` to retrieve results. This converts what should be one synchronous Bash call into 2–3 calls with artificial waits, repeated across 60+ invocations for trivial one-shot commands. The pattern massively inflates turn count and adds latency without providing any benefit over direct execution.

evidence_refs.excerpt: sleep 30 && cat /tmp/claude-1000/.../tasks/b4qi13jwa.output 2>/dev/null || echo "still waiting"
Monitor id=toolu_01149GgtPqdBuBJ3PtHBXgrn input: {"command": "grep -n \"keep_attrs\" /tmp/swebench/pydata__xarray-7229/xarray/core/common.py | head -50"}

patterns.description: Rather than decomposing the problem at its root, the agent patches every observed symptom in a single large post-processing block. The final xr.where edit is a 40+ line change that conflates lambda adjustment, result.attrs restoration, Dataset-vs-DataArray branching, coordinate-attr restoration, and data_vars-attr restoration — re-implementing attr-merging semantics xarray internals already provide, rather than fixing the coordinate-merging path in apply_dataarray_vfunc/apply_dataset_vfunc.

The agent searches git history (across all branches) for prior commits touching the affected code, discovers the upstream fix commit for the exact issue, retrieves the canonical patch via `git show`, and transcribes it nearly verbatim as its own solution. This short-circuits the agent's own derivation: even where earlier reasoning was on the right track, the final committed code is a direct copy of the reference implementation obtained by git-log archaeology rather than independent synthesis.

evidence_refs.excerpt: Now I have the reference fix. Let me implement the same approach.
git show 675a3ff6 -- xarray/core/computation.py
675a3ff6 Fix coordinate attr handling in `xr.where(..., keep_attrs=True)` (#7229)
git log --all --oneline -- xarray/tests/test_computation.py | grep -i "where_attr\|where attr\|keep_attr"

patterns.description: In the onlycode arm (where the Edit tool is unavailable), the agent modifies production source by writing multi-hundred-line Python scripts that do `content.replace(old, new)` or slice `lines[start:end]` with hardcoded line indices (e.g. 1859–1881). Several of these failed silently due to whitespace mismatches or shifting line indices between edits, and one splice accidentally dropped the `return result` statement — causing execution to fall through to the original unmodified `return apply_ufunc(...)` and reproduce the exact bug the fix was supposed to eliminate. The edit-by-script approach made rewrites unauditable and was the direct cause of multiple self-inflicted buggy intermediate states.

The agent makes multiple successive edits to the same code region, each revising the previous approach as new edge cases (Dataset variable attrs, scalar-x, explicit-override callers) surface. The churn reflects that the agent did not reason through the full input matrix before committing to an edit, and it only stabilizes on a final approach after consulting an external reference. Intermediate attempts (e.g. `keep_attrs = "override"` with post-hoc restoration, then a `_keep_x_attrs` flag) are discarded wholesale rather than incrementally refined.

patterns.description: The agent assumes properties of the runtime environment (which Python interpreter has the target package, whether `pip`/`pytest` are on PATH, which site-packages are active) without first probing, and only discovers each mistaken assumption after a failed invocation. Each assumption is corrected reactively turn-by-turn rather than being established up front with a single environment discovery step.

evidence_refs.excerpt: keep_attrs = lambda attrs, context: (x_attrs if any(a is x_attrs for a in attrs) else attrs[0] if attrs else {})

patterns.description: After applying code edits, the agent fails to execute the actual target tests that would confirm behavioral correctness. Instead it substitutes weaker surrogates — visual diff comparison against a reference commit, `inspect.getsource` dumps, or ad-hoc AST shape probes — and declares the fix verified despite never successfully running the relevant test. In both observed cases, environment issues (numpy 2.x incompatibility, missing pytest/astroid in the default interpreter) were encountered and then bypassed rather than resolved, leaving runtime verification skipped entirely.
